title: Myrtoleucodendron armillare
intro: Myrtoleucodendron armillare, also known as the "Munirivine", is a plant species within the family Rutaceae, native to tropical regions of Australia and New Guinea. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of M. armillare include flavonoids, coumarins, and limonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with M. armillare include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various health conditions and support overall well-being.
taxonomy: Myrtoleucodendron armillare is a species of flowering plant in the family Sapindaceae. It is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant is a member of the Sapindaceae family, which includes other species like the soapberry and lychee trees. Myrtoleucodendron armillare is further classified within the genus Myrtoleucodendron, which comprises several species of plants with showy flowers and woody stems. This classification suggests that Myrtoleucodendron armillare is a distinct species within the Sapindaceae family.
